ontology.foodex2_description: Stir-fried mixed dish with meat and carbohydrate base
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for stir-fried pork with noodles or rice; A0CKP (composite dish with meat and starchy base) used as closest defensible analogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of stir-fried pork with noodles or rice as typically served at a Singaporean hawker centre or coffee shop, approximately 350 g.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-Fried Pork With Noodles Or Rice — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Stir-Fried Pork With Noodles Or Rice (cr_id G00010080). All per-100g values are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried pork with noodles or rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (GI ~64, University of Sydney GI Database) and egg noodles (GI ~40–47, University of Sydney GI Database). A blended estimate weighted toward white rice as the more common base yields approximately GI 56. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 25.19 × 3.5) / 100 = 49.4 g per 350 g plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; a 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, representing ~35% of the 2300 mg daily limit, partially offset by high potassium (327 mg/100 g, ~1145 mg per serving) consistent with DASH diet principles.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request low-sodium soy sauce and oyster sauce, limit portion size to one standard plate, and monitor cumulative daily sodium intake from other meals to stay within the 2300 mg/day threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g; the medium GI (~56) and moderate glycaemic load (~49 per serving) require portion control and carbohydrate awareness.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should choose brown rice or wholegrain noodles over refined versions to lower the glycaemic response, keep portions to one standard plate, and account for the ~88 g total carbohydrate per 350 g serving within their daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 4.21 g/100 g with saturated fat data unavailable; pork and stir-fry oil contribute saturated and potentially trans fats; sodium at 231 mg/100 g adds cardiovascular risk at typical serving sizes.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request lean pork cuts and minimal oil during preparation, use low-sodium condiments, and pair with additional vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and fibre profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a red meat containing arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine production relevant to rheumatoid arthritis pathophysiology.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should replace pork with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as salmon, tofu, or legumes, retain the mixed vegetables for their antioxidant contribution, and use low-sodium soy sauce to reduce RA-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Pork With Noodles Or Rice is a popular Singaporean hawker and home-cooked dish consisting of sliced or minced pork stir-fried in a wok with either egg noodles or steamed white rice, seasoned with soy sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and sometimes dark soy sauce for colour. The dish is cooked over high heat using the wok hei technique, producing a lightly charred, savoury flavour with tender pork and a slightly chewy noodle or fluffy rice base. Mixed vegetables such as bean sprouts, cabbage, or spring onions are commonly added. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The moderate dietary fibre content slows glucose absorption, making it a borderline-suitable option for type 2 diabetes with appropriate carbohydrate management. The sodium level requires attention for hypertension patients, though the high potassium content (327 mg/100 g) partially offsets cardiovascular sodium risk in line with DASH diet principles. Pork as a red meat contributes arachidonic acid, which is pro-inflammatory and relevant for rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is a staple of Chinese-Singaporean cuisine, commonly served at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and home kitchens across Singapore for lunch and dinner.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010080) at confidence 0.95. The dish is a canonical Singaporean hawker item with an exact match in the primary regional approved source. GI is estimated from analogous approved-source components (white rice and egg noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database) due to absence of a direct composite GI measurement.
notes: saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite stir-fried dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry G00010080 as of 2026-04-16. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond the reported fields are not available in the HPB Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB; this is the source-reported value and is retained as confirmed zero per HPB Foodo. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on the dominant carbohydrate components (white rice GI ~64 and egg noodles GI ~40–47) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct composite GI measurement exists for this dish. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (56 × 25.19 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 49.4 g. The dish contains pork and is not halal-certified; halal suitability depends on preparation context.
